---
title: "Table of Correlations"
format: html
---

The second table you will probably create in your manuscript is the table of intercorrelations. I use the **apaTables** package to do this.

-   apaTables [@apaTables2026]

```{r}
#| label: Simulated data
#| include: false
set.seed(1234)

N <- 500

# Shared latent factor creates correlations between scales
general <- rnorm(N)

data <- data.frame(
  trust    = 4 + 0.60 * general + rnorm(N, 0, 0.80),
  identity = 4 + 0.70 * general + rnorm(N, 0, 0.80),
  efficacy = 4 + 0.65 * general + rnorm(N, 0, 0.80),
  action   = 4 + 0.75 * general + rnorm(N, 0, 0.80)
)

# Keep values roughly on a 1-7 scale
data <- data %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), ~pmin(pmax(., 1), 7)))

library(tidyverse)
```

```{r}
#| label: apaTables()
library(apaTables)
corr_table <- apa.cor.table(data[, c("trust", "identity", "efficacy", "action")], 
                        table.number = 2, 
                        show.sig.stars = TRUE, 
                        landscape = TRUE,
                        filename = "output/Table 2 Correlations pooled sample.docx")
```

**What this is doing:**

-   `corr_table <-` saves the created table to an object in your R environment.

-   `data[, c("var1", "var2", "var3", "var4")]` selects the variables to include, otherwise it will produce a correlations table for every variable in the dataframe.

-   `table.number = 2` allows you to set the number of the table in the produced artifact.

-   `landscape = TRUE` is pretty self-explanatory, it creates the table in a landscape oriented Word document. This might not be necessary if you only have a few variables; in this case, set this to `FALSE`.

-   `filename = "output/Table 2 Correlations pooled sample.doc"` saves the table to a word document in your 'outputs' folder in your R project.

You can then call it in your R environment using the code `corr_table`.

```{r}
corr_table
```

And the formatted Word document will produce a table like this:

![](/images/apaTables corr table.JPG)
